result:
This confirms a clean, real pattern in this Redis source tree. Summary:

## Redis string type (t_string.c) — no ops table, no switch even

- `t_string.c` never dispatches on `obj->encoding` via `switch`. It only has plain `if (o->encoding == OBJ_ENCODING_INT)` checks (e.g. lines 663, 917, 1315, 1655), because a string has just 3 encodings (`RAW`, `EMBSTR`, `INT`), and `RAW`/`EMBSTR` are both sds-backed so most code paths don't even need to branch — only the integer-shared-object fast path does.

## object.c — encoding dispatch is switch/if-else on an int tag, inline in the function body

Grep confirms 4 `switch(o->encoding)` sites plus assorted `if/else`, all encoded as literal tag comparisons compiled directly into the caller, not indirected through a struct of function pointers:
- `object.c:434` `dupStringObject()` — `switch(o->encoding)`: `OBJ_ENCODING_RAW` / `EMBSTR` / `INT` → different `create*` calls inline.
- `object.c:567` `freeSetObject()` — `switch(o->encoding)`: `HT` / `INTSET`+`LISTPACK`.
- `object.c:586` `freeZsetObject()` — `switch(o->encoding)`: `SKIPLIST` / `LISTPACK`.
- `object.c:1290` `strEncoding()` — `switch(encoding)` → string literal per tag (debug/introspection only).
- `object.c:556` `freeListObject()` — plain `if/else` (`QUICKLIST` vs `LISTPACK`), same style, no switch keyword.

Every `default:`/`else` branch is `serverPanic(...)` — a closed, exhaustively-known tag set, which is exactly the shape that favors switch-on-tag over indirection.

## The one genuine function-pointer ops table in this codebase: `moduleType` / `RedisModuleTypeMethods`

- Defined at `server.h:955` (`typedef struct RedisModuleType { ... } moduleType;`) and mirrored in the public API at `redismodule.h:1051` (`RedisModuleTypeMethods`). Fields are ~15 function pointers: `rdb_load`, `rdb_save`, `aof_rewrite`, `mem_usage`(+`2`), `digest`, `free`, `free_effort`(+`2`), `unlink`(+`2`), `copy`(+`2`), `defrag`, `aux_load`, `aux_save`(+`2`).
- This table is populated once at module registration (`module.c:7568-7589`, `RM_CreateDataType` copying a caller-supplied `RedisModuleTypeMethods` into a heap-allocated `moduleType`), then *called through* at every use site:
  - `object.c:607` `mv->type->free(mv->value)`
  - `rdb.c:1500` `mt->rdb_save(&io,mv->value)`, `rdb.c:1716/1731` `aux_save2/aux_save`, `rdb.c:3761` `mt->rdb_load(...)`, `rdb.c:4287` `mt->aux_load(...)`
  - `module.c:7394/7396` `mt->copy2(...)` / `mt->copy(...)`
- This table exists **only for `OBJ_MODULE`** — the escape hatch modules use to register arbitrary custom data types (`RM_CreateDataType`). It is not used for any of the five built-in types (string/list/hash/set/zset).

## The distinction (this repo)

- **Built-in types** (`OBJ_STRING`/`LIST`/`HASH`/`SET`/`ZSET`): encoding is a closed `int` enum (`OBJ_ENCODING_*`), dispatch is direct `switch`/`if-else` on `obj->encoding`, resolved and inlined at compile time inside `object.c`/`t_string.c` — one level of indirection (the tag comparison itself), no indirect call.
- **Module-defined types** (`OBJ_MODULE`): dispatch is through `moduleValue->type->fn(...)`, a genuine runtime-populated struct-of-function-pointers analogous to wpa_supplicant's ops-table pattern — two levels of indirection (load `mt` pointer from the object, then indirect-call through the field), because the set of implementations is open-ended (third-party modules) and unknown at compile time.

So: no, redis's string type does **not** use function-pointer dispatch — only the module subsystem does, and it does so precisely because that's the one place where the "type" isn't a closed compile-time enum. Per the graph's blind-spot note, edges into `moduleType` function-pointer fields are struct-field indirection and won't show as direct call edges in ccodegraph — confirmed above by reading the registration site (`module.c:7568-7589`) and each call site directly rather than trusting a heuristic `fnptr` edge.
